---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-worldmap3.png')
background-size: 105%
background-class: top

.center[.size50[**IV. What is the Future of Transnational Politics and IR?**]]

<br>

.size50[
**Today's Agenda**

- Explore the current state of human rights conditions around the world
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Fall 2021)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Check the Google sheet for participation

2. Data: Consider a few slides presenting data on their chosen cases to aid discussion of the sample

3. Spreadsheet with CIRIGHTS data for selected countries




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[.content-box-purple[**Assignment for Next Class**]]

.size35[Choose two countries: a democracy and a dictatorship (claim yours on Google Sheet, first-come, first-served). 

Review the current human rights record for both countries using all three sources below. 

1. The country reports by the US Department of State
2. The country reports by Amnesty International
3. The country reports by Human Rights Watch

Submit a summary of each country to Canvas before class (2-3 sentences each)
]

???

For today I asked each of you to perform two case studies that would help us think about the state of human rights in the world.

### Everybody ready to go?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size60[.content-box-white[**A Case Study**]]

.size55[
"A case study is an .textblue[**in-depth examination**], often undertaken over time, of .textblue[**a single case**] – such as a policy, programme, intervention site, implementation process or participant" (Goodrick 2014, 1).
]

???

Your work for today is what we refer to as a "case study."

- Although, to be fair to what we did, we did a very brief case study at a single moment in recent time...

- Still, I think you get the gist.

<br>

**SLIDE**: Thanks to all of your work before class, we can now use our time in class to perform a comparative case analysis.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size60[.content-box-white[**A Comparative Case Study**]]

.size45[
"Comparative case studies involve the .textblue[**analysis and synthesis of the similarities, differences and patterns across two or more cases**] that share a common focus or goal in a way that .textblue[**produces knowledge**] that is easier to generalize about causal questions – how and why particular programmes or policies work or fail to work" (Goodrick 2014).
]

???

In other words, comparing all of your cases allows us to:

1. Broaden our descriptive knowledge of the world,

2. Identify puzzles that need some explanation (generates new research projects), and

3. Develop generalizations about how certain mechanisms or processes might work.

<br>

I'm hoping we can do some of all three of those things today.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/15_1-Sources.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

*NOTE TO YOU: Shorten discussion of bias in the sources*

<br>

Before we dig into the cases, let's talk about our data sources.

- Heading into areas we know nothing about puts even more pressure on us to build knowledge using "high quality" sources.

<br>

I'll be totally honest with you here: Each of these sources has its own problems

- The US State Dept is explicitly a political entity with a very specific perspective

- Both AI and HRW are NGOs that face funding pressures and must be careful to ensure access to the places they want to do work in.

<br>

### So, what is our best bet for establishing "high confidence" facts from these three sources?

(Most confident where all three reports agree with each other?)
- This assumes all three asking the same question, right?

(Least confident when:)
- only one mentions something,
- when a "fact" lies at the heart of the source's bias,




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[.center[.content-box-white[**Compare and contrast the democracy cases**]]]

.size40[
+ In general, how good is the respect for human rights in these cases?
	+ Excellent
	+ Good
	+ Fair
	+ Poor

+ Most targeted groups?

+ Most violated rights?
]

???

*Split into groups of four*

Try to work with new people!

- Your job is to compare and contrast the democracy cases and get ready to report back your findings.

<br>

### Ok, any early conclusions we can draw at this point about human rights practices in democracies?

*Report Back and Discuss*

- *ON BOARD*




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size50[.center[.content-box-white[**Compare and contrast the dictatorship cases**]]]

.size40[
+ In general, how good is the respect for human rights in these cases?
	+ Excellent
	+ Good
	+ Fair
	+ Poor

+ Most targeted groups?

+ Most violated rights?
]

???

Mix up the groups!

- Go work with new people!

<br>

Groups, compare and contrast the dictatorship cases and get ready to report back your findings.

<br>

### Ok, any early conclusions we can draw at this point about human rights practices in dictatorships?

*Report Back and Discuss*

- *ON BOARD*






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12_1-human-rights-vdem.svg')
background-size: 80%
background-position: center

???

Ok, that said, let's compare and contrast across our cases.

### Based on this comparative case study how comfortable are you in asserting that regime type is a predictor of human rights behavior?

### - Why or why not?

<br>

### - Do the regime types differ significantly in terms of their most common "human rights" violations?

<br>

### - Do the regime types differ significantly in terms of who specifically in the country is doing the harms?

<br>

### - Do the regime types differ significantly in terms of how the victims are being targeted?

<br>

### - Any evidence in the cases of any of these identified harms being addressed?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12_1-Team_America.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Last big question for us today.

### Do you think human rights violations should compel American action?

### - Should we be the world's policeman when human rights are under assault? Why or why not?


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/12_1-Fragile_States2023.png')
background-size: 70%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

The question might not be so cut and dry.

<br>

Here we see the Fragile States Index.

- Some have demonstrated that states with increasing levels of human rights violations raise their risk of failure (e.g. governmental collapse).

- And state failures tend not to stay isolated within their borders, e.g. one failed state tends to destabilize those around them.

- Therefore, preventing small conflicts from becoming wider wars may require earlier interference in states when human rights violations increase.

<br>

### Are you buying that logic? Why or why not?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[.content-box-white[**Assignment for Next Class**]]

<br>

.size45[
1. Krain (2012) p574-578

2. **Before class** submit to our Canvas discussion board: 
    - What is the research question in this paper?
    - What is their proposed answer to the question?
]

???

For the rest of this week we'll be digging deeply into our next research article of the semester!

- For Wed, your job is to read the set-up of the paper (574-578) so we can explore the model he proposes to test.

- Friday we'll dig into the data analyses.

<br>

### Questions on the assignment?
